content.full_text:
March 2, 1992


James F. Meade
Deputy County Counsel
County of Orange
10 Civic Center Plaza
Post Office Box 1379
Santa Ana, CA  92702-1379

                                        Re:  Your Request for Informal Assistance
                                             Our File No. I-91-533

Dear Mr. Meade:

     You have requested advice on behalf of Michael M. Ruane,
Director of the Orange County Environmental Management Agency, and
the employees of that agency concerning their duties under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act").1  Because your request seeks general guidance about the
agency's course of conduct in connection with a proposed
contractual relationship, we are treating your request as one for
informal assistance.2  Please note that the Commission's
jurisdiction is limited to the provisions of the Act. Other law
which may be applicable to your facts, such as case law
interpreting the California Environmental Quality Act, the
Community Redevelopment Law, or the Community Planning and
Development Law, are outside the jurisdiction of the Commission.

                              QUESTION

     The county and a developer are in negotiations regarding the
sale of a parcel of county-owned property.  The proposed sale is
contingent upon county approval of a general plan and zoning

_________________________
1  Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2  Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-91-533
Page 2


amendments. The payment of fees by the developer also is
contingent upon county approval of the general plan and zoning
amendments as well as upon the consummation of the sale.

May the county enter into an agreement with the developer
containing terms which provide that the developer's payment of
fees to the county to defray the costs of processing the general
plan and zoning amendments is contingent upon agency approval or
disapproval of these applications and upon the result of agency
action?

CONCLUSION

We do not have enough facts to make a determination as to
whether such a contingency arrangement that you describe would be
permitted under Section 87103.6. Compliance with Regulation
18704.6 will clearly avoid a determination that the developer fees
are a source of income to agency employees and consultants paid
from those fees. However, we recognize that this advice may be
conservative and may change when the Commission reconsiders the
provisions of Regulation 18704.6 in light of Section 87103.6.

FACTS

The Orange County Environmental Management Agency (the
"Agency") is negotiating an agreement for payment of fees with a
developer in connection with the sale of property. Specifically,
the county is negotiating the sale of a parcel of county-owned
property to a developer. The proposed sale is contingent upon
county approval of a general plan and zoning amendments.

The payment of fees by the developer also is contingent upon
county approval of the general plan and zoning amendments as well
as upon the consummation of the sale. The proposed agreement
would require the developer to pay the costs of processing the
general plan and zoning amendments, but only if these applications
are approved by the county, and only if the sale is consummated.
The county has no contractual commitment to approve the
applications.

It is anticipated that only full-time county employees will
process the applications, but it is possible that the county may
need to retain consultants for this purpose as well.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making,
participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official
position to influence a governmental decision in which the
official has a financial interest. A "public official" is defined
in Regulation 18700 to include every natural person who is a
member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local
government agency. (Section 82048.) This definition would
include Agency employees and in all likelihood will also include

Our File No. A-91-533
Page 3


persons who are retained under contract by the Agency to process
applications in connection with this developer's project.

     Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a
member of his or her immediate family or on:

     (c) Any source of income aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

     Therefore, as a general rule, any person or business that has
made any payment to a public official in the past 12 months is a
source of income to that official for the purposes of Section
87103(c). However, Section 82030(b)(2) provides an exception for
certain types of payments which are made to agency employees and
to certain consultants who are under contract to the agency.3
Specifically, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem
received from a public agency are exempted from the definition of
"income" and are, therefore, not considered "sources of income" to
agency employees and to certain consultants retained by an agency.

     The recently enacted Section 87103.6 provides another
limited exception:

          Notwithstanding subdivision (c) of Section
     87103, any person who makes a payment to a state
     agency or local government agency to defray the
     estimated reasonable costs to process any
     application, approval, or any other action,
     including but not limited to, holding public
     hearings and evaluating or preparing any report or
     document, shall not by reason of the payments be a
     source of income to a person who is retained or
     employed by the agency.

     Therefore, generally speaking, any person who makes a payment
to a public agency to defray processing costs is exempted from the
definition of "source" and shall not by reason of the payments be
a "source of income" to an employee or a consultant who is
retained or employed by the agency.

     Approximately 10 days prior to the enactment of Section
87103.6, the Commission had adopted Regulation 18704.6.
Regulation 18704.6 addressed the same problem as the statute and

_____________________________
     3 Only consultants who are public officials as that term is
defined in the Act are subject to the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Act.

Our File No. A-91-533
Page 4


also created a limited exemption for persons making payments to
public agencies for the purpose of defraying costs. The
regulation is narrow in scope and expressly establishes criteria
that must be met before the exemption may be applied. One of
these criteria is that the payment of fees should not be
contingent upon the agency's approval or disapproval of any
application or upon the result of any other agency action. In
your situation, the proposed contract language expressly contains
such a contingency payment. The agency will either refund or
waive normal processing fees if the agency does not approve the
developer's application for a general plan and zoning amendments
on the subject property (and if the sale of the agency's property
is not consummated). Your situation directly raises an inquiry as
to the continued viability and applicability of Regulation 18704.6
in view of Section 87103.6.

You contend that the proposed contingency arrangement is
permissible under the statute. This contention is premised on
your interpretation of Section 87103.6, which you state
"categorically exempts" persons making such payments from the
definition of "source of income" within the meaning of the Act.
Because Regulation 18704.6 sets forth certain conditions for the
exemption to apply, you argue it is in irreconcilable conflict
with the newly enacted statute. We disagree in part and agree in
part.

Underlying the conflict-of-interest provisions is one of the
most fundamental purposes of the Act: "Public officials, whether
elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial
manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or
the financial interests of persons who have supported them."
(Section 81001(b).) It would be quite anomalous for the Act, a
statute designed to prevent public officials from participating in
decisions in which they have a financial interest, to permit a
narrow exception to become a broad loophole.

An obvious conflict of interest arises if the consultant
retained by the public agency to process the developer's
applications has a financial tie to the developer. This situation
may occur, for example, if the payment of fees to an agency is
dependent upon the agency's selection, hiring, or retention of
certain individual employees or consultants. There also may be
other contingency payment situations which were not intended to be
exempted under the statute because they lend themselves to biased
conduct.

We believe that most of the provisions of Regulation 18704.6
are consistent with the provisions of Section 87103.6, and can be
reconciled with a narrow interpretation of that statute. Such a
narrow interpretation is warranted if Section 87103.6 is to be
viewed as furthering the purposes of the Act. (Section 81014.)
On the other hand, we agree with you that not all of the
provisions of the regulation may continue to remain viable. One
of these provisions is the blanket prohibition on contingency

Our File No. A-91-533
Page 5


payments between the agency and the person paying the fees to
defray his or her processing costs.4  Staff will be recommending
that this prohibition be narrowed.

Regulation 18704.6 has not been formally codified subsequent
to its adoption by the Commission, because of the passage of
election 87103.6.  This being the case, there is no express
prohibition under the Act prohibiting you from entering into such
a contingency arrangement.  However, the regulation does represent
the expression of Commission policy on the issue of developer
fees/sources of income exception.  Clearly, the proposed
contingency arrangement fails to comply with the terms of the
regulation as adopted.  Moreover, we believe that the statute
could be interpreted to preclude the proposed contingency
arrangement under certain circumstances.

We do not have enough facts to make a determination in your
case as to whether the arrangement you present would violate
Section 87103.6.  Therefore, our conservative advice is that
adherence to the rules enunciated by the Commission in connection
with its prior adoption of Regulation 18704.6 will avoid a "source
of income" problem pending clarification of Section 87103.6.

We recognize that the Commission may elect not to adopt the
limited language concerning contingency fee arrangements set forth
above or to delete the provision in its entirety.  In that
eventuality, it is our understanding that contingent fee
arrangements may pose similar issues that are governed by case law
interpreting other statutory schemes, such as the California
Environmental Quality Act, the Community Redevelopment Law, or the
Community Planning and Development Law.  However, apart from the
bias of public officials caused by their own financial interests,
other possible biases in processing applications, approvals, or
any other agency actions, is not covered by the Act and is,
therefore, not within our jurisdiction.

As indicated above, in view of the passage of Senate Bill 883
and Section 87103.6, staff will be recommending that revisions be

_______________
4  This provision currently reads as follows:

     (4) The agreement entered into between the agency
     and the person paying the fees or making the
     payments shall be reduced to writing and shall:

                    *  *  *

     (C) Not be dependent upon the agency's
     approval or disapproval of the application or upon
     the result of any agency action ...

                    *  *  *

Our File No. A-91-533
Page 6


made to Regulation 18704.6. A copy of the proposed amendments to
the regulation are attached. Staff invites your comments to the
proposed revised regulation.

If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.5

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

Deanne Stone

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DRS:aa

cc: Michael M. Ruane, Director
Environmental Management Agency
County of Orange
Post Office Box 4048
Santa Ana, CA 92702-4048




5 Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.

sections.facts:
The Orange County Environmental Management Agency (the "Agency") is negotiating an agreement for payment of fees with a developer in connection with the sale of property. Specifically, the county is negotiating the sale of a parcel of county-owned property to a developer. The proposed sale is contingent upon county approval of a general plan and zoning amendments.

The payment of fees by the developer also is contingent upon county approval of the general plan and zoning amendments as well as upon the consummation of the sale. The proposed agreement would require the developer to pay the costs of processing the general plan and zoning amendments, but only if those applications are approved by the county, and only if the sale is consummated. The county has no contractual commitment to approve the applications.

It is anticipated that only full-time county employees will process the applications, but it is possible that the county may need to retain consultants for this purpose as well.
sections.analysis:
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. A "public official" is defined in Regulation 18700 to include every natural person who is a member, officer, employee, or consultant of a state or local government agency. (Section 82048.) This definition would include Agency employees and in all likelihood will also include

persons who are retained under contract by the Agency to process applications in connection with this developer's project.

Section 87103 specifies that an official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the public generally, on the official or a member of his or her immediate family or on:

(c) Any source of income aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided to, received by, or promised to the public official within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made.

Therefore, as a general rule, any person or business that has made any payment to a public official in the past 12 months is a source of income to that official for the purposes of Section 87103(c). However, Section 82030(b)(2) provides an exception for certain types of payments which are made to agency employees and to certain consultants who are under contract to the agency.3 Specifically, salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a public agency are exempted from the definition of "income" and are, therefore, not considered "sources of income" to agency employees and to certain consultants retained by an agency.

The recently enacted Section 87103.6 provides another limited exception:

Notwithstanding subdivision (c) of Section 87103, any person who makes a payment to a state agency or local government agency to defray the estimated reasonable costs to process any application, approval, or any other action, including but not limited to, holding public hearings and evaluating or preparing any report or document, shall not by reason of the payments be a source of income to a person who is retained or employed by the agency.

Therefore, generally speaking, any person who makes a payment to a public agency to defray processing costs is exempted from the definition of "source" and shall not by reason of the payments be a "source of income" to an employee or a consultant who is retained or employed by the agency.

Approximately 10 days prior to the enactment of Section 87103.6, the Commission had adopted Regulation 18704.6. Regulation 18704.6 addressed the same problem as the statute and

3 Only consultants who are public officials as that term is defined in the Act are subject to the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Act.

also created a limited exemption for persons making payments to public agencies for the purpose of defraying costs. The regulation is narrow in scope and expressly establishes criteria that must be met before the exemption may be applied. One of these criteria is that the payment of fees should not be contingent upon the agency's approval or disapproval of any application or upon the result of any other agency action. In your situation, the proposed contract language expressly contains such a contingency payment. The agency will either refund or waive normal processing fees if the agency does not approve the developer's application for a general plan and zoning amendments on the subject property (and if the sale of the agency's property is not consummated). Your situation directly raises an inquiry as to the continued viability and applicability of Regulation 18704.6 in view of Section 87103.6.

You contend that the proposed contingency arrangement is permissible under the statute. This contention is premised on your interpretation of Section 87103.6, which you state "categorically exempts" persons making such payments from the definition of "source of income" within the meaning of the Act. Because Regulation 18704.6 sets forth certain conditions for the exemption to apply, you argue it is in irreconcilable conflict with the newly enacted statute. We disagree in part and agree in part.

Underlying the conflict-of-interest provisions is one of the most fundamental purposes of the Act: "Public officials, whether elected or appointed, should perform their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) It would be quite anomalous for the Act, a statute designed to prevent public officials from participating in decisions in which they have a financial interest, to permit a narrow exception to become a broad loophole.

An obvious conflict of interest arises if the consultant retained by the public agency to process the developer's applications has a financial tie to the developer. This situation may occur, for example, if the payment of fees to an agency is dependent upon the agency's selection, hiring, or retention of certain individual employees or consultants. There also may be other contingency payment situations which were not intended to be exempted under the statute because they lend themselves to biased conduct.

We believe that most of the provisions of Regulation 18704.6 are consistent with the provisions of Section 87103.6, and can be reconciled with a narrow interpretation of that statute. Such a narrow interpretation is warranted if Section 87103.6 is to be viewed as furthering the purposes of the Act. (Section 81014.) On the other hand, we agree with you that not all of the provisions of the regulation may continue to remain viable. One of these provisions is the blanket prohibition on contingency

payments between the agency and the person paying the fees to defray his or her processing costs.4 Staff will be recommending that this prohibition be narrowed.

Regulation 18704.6 has not been formally codified subsequent to its adoption by the Commission, because of the passage of Section 87103.6. This being the case, there is no express prohibition under the Act prohibiting you from entering into such a contingency arrangement. However, the regulation does represent the expression of Commission policy on the issue of developer fees/sources of income exception. Clearly, the proposed contingency arrangement fails to comply with the terms of the regulation as adopted. Moreover, we believe that the statute could be interpreted to preclude the proposed contingency arrangement under certain circumstances.

We do not have enough facts to make a determination in your case as to whether the arrangement you present would violate Section 87103.6. Therefore, our conservative advice is that adherence to the rules enunciated by the Commission in connection with its prior adoption of Regulation 18704.6 will avoid a "source of income" problem pending clarification of Section 87103.6.

We recognize that the Commission may elect not to adopt the limited language concerning contingency fee arrangements set forth above or to delete the provision in its entirety. In that eventuality, it is our understanding that contingent fee arrangements may pose similar issues that are governed by case law interpreting other statutory schemes, such as the California Environmental Quality Act, the Community Redevelopment Law, or the Community Planning and Development Law. However, apart from the bias of public officials caused by their own financial interests, other possible biases in processing applications, approvals, or any other agency actions, is not covered by the Act and is, therefore, not within our jurisdiction.

As indicated above, in view of the passage of Senate Bill 883 and Section 87103.6, staff will be recommending that revisions be

2 Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

amendments. The payment of fees by the developer also is contingent upon county approval of the general plan and zoning amendments as well as upon the consummation of the sale.

May the county enter into an agreement with the developer containing terms which provide that the developer’s payment of fees to the county to defray the costs of processing the general plan and zoning amendments is contingent upon agency approval or disapproval of these applications and upon the result of agency action?

CONCLUSION: We do not have enough facts to make a determination as to whether such a contingency arrangement that you describe would be permitted under Section 87103.6. Compliance with Regulation 18704.6 will clearly avoid a determination that the developer fees are a source of income to agency employees and consultants paid from those fees. However, we recognize that this advice may be conservative and may change when the Commission reconsiders the provisions of Regulation 18704.6 in light of Section 87103.6.
